text: In China, BMW isn't BMW, it's bao ma, treasure horse. Mercedes is ben qi, galloping speed. And Coca-Cola, delicious happiness. None of those are accidents. When a brand enters China, it doesn't just copy the sound. It picks characters that sound right and mean something flattering. Here's how the biggest names did it and how Chinese brands now play the same game even harder. Start with the cars. BMW became bao ma. Bao means treasure. Ma means horse. It still sounds like BMW, but now it also promises you a prized, powerful steed. Mercedes-Benz became ben qi. It means to gallop, to speed. Sounds like Benz, means pure emotion. The name is the marketing and the all-time masterpiece. Coca-Cola, kei ko kei le. It copies the sound almost perfectly and it means tasty and enjoyable. A soda literally named delicious happiness. Plenty of brands pick a name for their image, but Chinese brands take it to another level, especially the new electric car makers. They're not translating a foreign sound at all. They just reach into the language and grab a word that is their mission. One brand is simply called li xiang. The whole name just means the ideal. Another you know in the West is Nio. In Chinese, it's wei lai, which sounds exactly like wei lai, the future. A car company named literally the future. And here's the kicker. They run the same play in English too. Take BYD. It actually stands for build your dreams. An entire aspiration baked right into the logo. Bold, a bit much? We'll let you be the judge. A treasure horse, a galloping star. Building your dreams? Every one of these names is a tiny Chinese lesson hiding in plain sight. That's branding as a free language lesson. Wanna actually start learning Chinese? And Coca-Cola, delicious happiness.
None of those are accidents.
When a brand enters China, it doesn't just copy the sound.
It picks characters that sound right
and mean something flattering.
Here's how the biggest names did it
and how Chinese brands now play the same game even harder.
Start with the cars.
BMW became bao ma.
Bao means treasure.
Ma means horse.

but now it also promises you a prized, powerful steed.

Coca-Cola, kei ko kei le.
It copies the sound almost perfectly
and it means tasty and enjoyable.
A soda literally named delicious happiness.

especially the new electric car makers.

In Chinese, it's wei lai,
which sounds exactly like wei lai, the future.

They run the same play in English too.
